cards.question: Which subshell fills first: 4s or 3d? State the rule.
cards.answer: 4s fills before 3d, because 4s is at a lower energy than 3d (electrons fill the lowest energy levels first — the Aufbau principle).
cards.explanation: But 4s is also emptied/ionised first — that catches people out for ion configurations.

cards.question: State Hund's rule for filling orbitals of equal energy.
cards.answer: Electrons occupy orbitals of the same energy singly (one each) before pairing up, and with parallel spins.
cards.explanation: This minimises repulsion. Relevant when drawing electrons-in-boxes diagrams.

cards.question: Write the full electron configuration of a calcium atom (Z = 20).
cards.explanation: 4s fills after 3p (and before 3d). Total electrons must sum to 20.

cards.question: Write the electron configuration of an iron(II) ion, Fe²⁺. [Fe is Z = 26]
cards.answer: Fe is 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 3d⁶ 4s². For Fe²⁺ remove the 4s electrons first: 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 3d⁶.
cards.explanation: The classic trap: when forming ions, the 4s electrons are lost before 3d.

cards.question: Chromium and copper are exceptions to the normal filling order. State their electron configurations (using [Ar] core).
cards.explanation: A half-full (3d⁵) or full (3d¹⁰) d subshell is extra stable, so one 4s electron promotes to 3d.

cards.question: Write the electron configuration of a chloride ion, Cl⁻. [Cl is Z = 17]
cards.explanation: Cl⁻ has gained one electron (18 total) — same configuration as argon.
